rank_note: Rank intentionally null. site/src/data/indexes/us-states.json contains only 21 of 51 jurisdictions (the top 8 and bottom 13 of the original ranking); ranks 9-38 were lost in the original legacy-HTML extraction and the survivors were renumbered contiguously, so the displayed rank column is not national rank and the published set is a bimodal artifact, not a representative distribution. Pennsylvania does not currently appear in the published index at all and must not be assigned a rank until the index is rebuilt with all 51 jurisdictions.
calibration_note: Not calibrated to published us-states rank order (known corrupt — the published file publishes only the top 8 and bottom 13 of the original ranking with a 58-point hole between rank 8 and rank 9; see rank_note). Calibrated to behavioral anchors directly (upper anchor Connecticut 83.0, dimensions 3.5-4.5; lower anchor Louisiana 21.9 and Alabama/Mississippi 12.5, dimensions 1.0-2.0) and against the other five states assessed at full depth on 2026-07-19: Georgia 26.9, Ohio 38.1, Michigan 51.9 (superseded, corrected 53.8), Delaware 53.8, Pennsylvania 54.4, New York 56.9. Pennsylvania is clearly below Connecticut (lacks a functioning civil-rights enforcement commission at quorum, a chronic budget impasse, a $7.25 minimum wage since 2009) and clearly above the bottom cluster (expanded Medicaid, funds an environmental-justice office with a 32-indicator screening tool, publishes disaggregated maternal mortality data, commissioned an independent third-party audit of its own state police). Composite verified with computeCompositeFromDimensions in site/scripts/lib/scoring.mjs: dimension mean 3.175, baseComposite 54.375, stdDev 0.3072 (consistencyMult 1.0), 8 of 8 dimensions below 4.0 so weaknessFactor 0 and integrationPremium 0. Function returns {"composite":54.4,"band":"Functional"} — matches the target exactly.
attribution_note: Scores Pennsylvania's own state conduct. Federal work-reporting requirements are projected to remove 310,000 Pennsylvanians from Medicaid beginning October 2026; this is scored as a federal imposition, not Pennsylvania conduct, and Pennsylvania's mitigation response was not yet evidenced at assessment time and is flagged for reassessment after October 2026 (EQ1 held at 4 rather than penalized for a federal cut not yet enacted by the state). By contrast, Pennsylvania's own responses to federal action are scored as state conduct: the state sued the federal administration over a claimed $2 billion funding freeze (filed 2025-02-13), over $13 million in terminated Local Food Purchase Assistance funds, over revoked Homeland Security funds, and joined 24 states challenging the November 2025 federal SNAP suspension, declaring a disaster emergency and releasing $5 million of its own funds to food banks — these are Pennsylvania's own discretionary choices to spend political capital and state money defending residents, and are scored positively (S4, I1). Pennsylvania's 135-day 2025-26 budget impasse, its $7.25 minimum wage held since 2009, the foster-care Social Security diversion, and the Human Relations Commission's fall below quorum are all entirely state-level failures with no federal component and are fully scored (ACT, INT, BND, ACC).
key_evidence: Pennsylvania is a genuinely mid-pack state: it scores 54.4, no dimension is failing and no dimension is excellent, with all 8 dimensions landing between 45 and 65 on the 0-100 scale — a flatness that costs it the methodology's consistency bonus entirely because the bonus only pays when every dimension clears 4.0 of 5.

Pennsylvania sees suffering well and repairs it poorly: it scores 65/100 on Awareness (its joint-best result, via tools like the 32-indicator PennEnviroScreen) but only 2/5 on Reparative Action, its worst subdimension — when a 2025 investigation found counties had taken at least $15.7 million in Social Security benefits from at least 1,300 foster children, the proposed fix (HB 151) only stops future diversion and returns nothing to the children.

The state's own legislature is the largest single source of harm identified in this assessment: the 2025-26 budget was 135 days late, the last in the nation, cutting off $5.3 billion owed to schools and forcing rape crisis centers, domestic violence programs and Head Start providers to cut services; the 2026-27 budget was late for a fifth consecutive year.

Pennsylvania's minimum wage has been $7.25 an hour since 2009 — the lowest of any neighboring state (New York $17, New Jersey $15.92, Maryland $15, Ohio $11, West Virginia $8.75) — a 17-year gap between stated poverty-reduction values and delivered policy.

Pennsylvania's most important civil-rights protection is structurally fragile: its June 2023 ban on sexual-orientation and gender-identity discrimination exists only by Human Relations Commission regulation, not statute, is under constitutional challenge, and the Commission itself has received no new commissioner appointments since 2018 and has fallen below the quorum it needs to act.

Pennsylvania defends its residents from federal harm more reliably than from its own: it sued over a federal funding freeze and joined 24 states challenging the November 2025 SNAP suspension affecting nearly 2 million residents, declaring a disaster emergency and releasing $5 million in state funds, while its own budget impasse was simultaneously defunding domestic violence programs.

People who had been approved for benefits in the old computer system got rejected by the new one, and other people were misidentified as incarcerated in the system, and therefore ineligible to receive benefits.

After holding 11 public hearings around the state in fall 2023, the commission issued a majority report on Jan. 11, 2024, finding that Pennsylvania school districts are underfunded by more than $5.4 billion and recommending a seven-year plan.

The independent analysis found no substantive racial or ethnic disparities in the reasons for stops and no substantive differences in post-stop outcomes (warnings, citations and arrests) for Black and Hispanic drivers. The 2024 analysis included 433,599 traffic stops.
Report: PA trooper traffic stops similar regardless of driver's race.

Nearly every county in the state took Social Security money intended for children in their care, with youth often not directly notified their money had been taken, suggesting a possible due process violation.
Proactive Community Engagement: describes ways to increase proactive outreach and engagement across Pennsylvania and build long-lasting relationships with communities outside of individual projects.

The budget impasse lasted 135 days, finally ending when Gov. Josh Shapiro signed a $50.09 billion state budget into law on November 12. Schools, counties, and social service providers received four months of withheld state payments.
PA's budget is late for the 5th year in a row.

Since Governor Josh Shapiro's historic investment of $354.8 million in federal and state funding secured in the 2024-25 budget, there has been a 31 percent reduction in the ID/A adult emergency waiting list for services.
The waivers currently require that people in the emergency category are enrolled into a waiver before those in the critical or planning categories.

A website launched in August 2025 publishes and tracks opioid settlement fund spending data in Pennsylvania. Over $80 million had been spent on approved opioid remediation programs as of December 31, 2024.
Pennsylvania's drug overdose deaths fell to about 3,300 in 2024, representing a significant decline from about 4,700 such deaths in the state in 2023.
Pennsylvania's current state minimum wage of $7.25 hasn't been changed since 2009. Pennsylvania, at $7.25, has the lowest hourly minimum wage within this group of states.
An additional $565 million would be directed toward adequacy and tax equity on top of the $3 billion currently spent.
These successes are thanks to the Shapiro Administration's multi-year growth strategy for ID/A services and continued investments in the 2025-26 state budget.
Recommending a seven-year plan for the state to close this massive funding gap.

Under the governor's spending plan, an additional $565 million would be directed toward adequacy and tax equity on top of the $3 billion currently spent.

In 2021, 69.9 Black/African American women and birthing individuals per 100,000 live births died from maternal causes, versus 26.6 of their white counterparts — a rate 2.6 times higher.
Independent analysis led by Dr. Robin Engel of the John Glenn College of Public Affairs found no substantive racial or ethnic disparities.

Act 36 of 2023 expands Pennsylvania's landmark Clean Slate initiative and establishes a process for automatic expungement of criminal records, extending automatic expungement eligibility to certain non-violent felony offenses.

Pennsylvania's new nursing home staffing regulations went into effect on July 1, 2023, requiring nursing home patients to receive 2.87 hours of direct care per day, up from 2.7 hours. Effective July 1, 2024, the requirement increased to 3.2 hours.

In 2010 the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania (joined by the Pennhurst Memorial and Preservation Alliance and the Public Interest Law Center of Philadelphia) dedicated a historical marker to Pennhurst's enduring legacy.

Pennhurst's founding and early operation cannot be separated from the eugenics movement. The institution was explicitly designed to segregate individuals deemed 'defective' from the broader population.

Eligible Commonwealth employees can take up to eight weeks of paid parental leave, an increase from the previous six weeks offered.
